label: verify-of-san-buenaventura-v-united-water-conse
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist

## How the documents used it

[tetlock] …iered rates are common utility practice and are often defensible as system-wide demand-management costs (reduced peaking/capacity needs benefit all ratepayers, including payors), which cuts against a bright-line rule.
- City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191 and Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 support a 'reasonable relationship'/no-mathematical-precision standard, giving agencies some latitude to pool revenue ac…

[dialectic] …ate structure entirely within the § 6(b) framework. The District cannot distinguish its own surcharge from the fact pattern its opponents are citing against it.
- **Distinguish the District's likely counter-citation.** *City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist.* (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191 held that a *groundwater extraction/pumping* charge imposed on extractors (not directly on property owners as retail customers) is not a property-related fee under Art. XIII D and is instead anal…

[naive_fable] … to parcels is the paradigm case. The authorities the district would invoke are distinguishable: *Richmond v. Shasta CSD* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409 involves connection fees (voluntary acts, not incidents of ownership), and *City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist.* (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191 involves groundwater *extraction* charges on pumpers, not retail delivery. Note also art. XIII D, § 3(b) exempts electricity and gas from the property-related-fee regime — conspicuously **not** w…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191","note":"Confirmed: the California Supreme Court held that a groundwater pumping/extraction charge imposed on well operators (extractors, not retail property owners) is not a \"property-related fee\" under Art. XIII D and instead is evaluated under Art. XIII C (Prop. 26)'s reasonable-relationship-to-burden/benefit standard, remanding for that analysis. This matches the dialectic and naive_fable snippets' use (distinguishing it as an extraction charge on pumpers, not a retail property-related fee). The tetlock snippet's use — invoking it for a general \"reasonable relationship/no-mathematical-precision\" latitude for tiered retail rates under Art. XIII D — is a looser, more generalized extension of the case's actual holding (which addressed threshold applicability of XIII D vs. XIII C to a different kind of charge), but is not a misattribution of the case or cite.","source_urls":["https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/2017/s226036.html","https://www.downeybrand.com/legal-alerts/california-supreme-court-confirms-certain-groundwater-pumping-charges-are-outside-scope-of-prop-218/","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/city-san-buenaventura-v-united-water-conservation-district-34548"]}

data.canonical_cite: City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191
data.note: Confirmed: the California Supreme Court held that a groundwater pumping/extraction charge imposed on well operators (extractors, not retail property owners) is not a "property-related fee" under Art. XIII D and instead is evaluated under Art. XIII C (Prop. 26)'s reasonable-relationship-to-burden/benefit standard, remanding for that analysis. This matches the dialectic and naive_fable snippets' use (distinguishing it as an extraction charge on pumpers, not a retail property-related fee). The tetlock snippet's use — invoking it for a general "reasonable relationship/no-mathematical-precision" latitude for tiered retail rates under Art. XIII D — is a looser, more generalized extension of the case's actual holding (which addressed threshold applicability of XIII D vs. XIII C to a different kind of charge), but is not a misattribution of the case or cite.
